title: Fault-Tolerant Resource Comparison of Qudit and Qubit Encodings for Diagonal Quadratic Operators
authors: Samuel Godwood, Doğa Murat Kürkçüoğlu, Gabriel N. Perdue, Marina Maneyro, Alessandro Roggero
abstract: Finite local Hilbert-space truncations arise naturally in quantum simulations of lattice field theories and motivate qudit encodings, but their fault-tolerant advantage over qubit encodings remains unclear. We compare the non-Clifford cost of implementing quadratic diagonal evolutions, exemplified by $U=e^{-itφ_x^2}$ in a uniform field-amplitude discretization of a real scalar field, using either one logical $d$-level qudit or $n_b=\lceil \log_2 d\rceil$ logical qubits. We analyze two standard settings: product-formula simulation and LCU/block encoding, taking the resource metric to be the number of non-Clifford gates after synthesis into a discrete logical gate set. Because tight synthesis bounds for general single-qudit rotations are not known, we express the qudit constructions in terms of embedded two-level $SU(2)$ rotations and derive explicit finite-$d$ break-even conditions for their synthesis cost; these serve as compiler targets for when qudit encodings can outperform the qubit baseline. Within the constructive models studied here, product-formula implementations would require an exponentially stronger per-primitive synthesis advantage for qudits to win asymptotically, while in the LCU setting the qubit encoding is asymptotically cheaper in $d$. Nevertheless, the finite-$d$ threshold analysis identifies low dimensional regions in which qudits can yield meaningful constant-factor savings, particularly for LCU-based implementations. As a secondary analysis of the LCU construction, we use an idealized negligible-overhead qubit-qudit code-switching model to give an absolute $T$-count comparison, and reinterpret the savings as an allowable per-switch overhead budget.
summary: This paper investigates whether using higher-dimensional qudits can reduce the computational overhead of simulating scalar field theories compared to standard qubit encodings. By analyzing non-Clifford gate costs in both Trotter-based and LCU-based simulation regimes, the authors find that while qubits win asymptotically, qudits offer significant resource savings for small, finite dimensions. The work provides explicit thresholds for quantum compilers to determine when qudit encodings are advantageous.
main_problem: Comparing the fault-tolerant resource costs, specifically non-Clifford gate counts, between qudit and qubit encodings for implementing diagonal quadratic operators.
main_result: While qubit encodings are asymptotically superior, qudits can provide meaningful constant-factor savings in specific finite-dimensional windows, particularly in the LCU framework and time-dominated regimes.
method: The authors analyze two simulation paradigms (product-formula and LCU/block-encoding) using a resource metric of non-Clifford gate counts after synthesis into a discrete gate set.
model_or_system: The study focuses on the implementation of diagonal quadratic operators, such as the mass term $e^{-it\phi_x^2}$ in a discretized real scalar field theory using a uniform grid.
key_observables: Non-Clifford gate counts (T-count), rotation counts, and the ratio of resource costs between qudit and qubit encodings.
important_parameters: Local dimension $d$, number of qubits $n_b$, synthesis prefactor $a$, target precision $\epsilon$, and simulation time $t$.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes an idealized, negligible-overhead code-switching model between qubits and qudits and focuses on the synthesis cost of embedded $SU(2)$ rotations.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the break-even synthesis prefactor versus dimension; Figure 2 compares non-Clifford resources in precision-dominated and time-dominated regimes.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of qudit vs. qubit encoding, analyzes the product-formula regime, evaluates the LCU framework, performs a secondary analysis using a code-switching model, and concludes with synthesis targets for compilers.
why_interesting: It provides critical guidance for designing hardware-efficient quantum algorithms for lattice field theories by identifying specific dimensions where qudit-based architectures outperform standard qubits.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Break-even synthesis prefactor aLCU max (d, 3000, 10−6) for the fixed-encoding Regime 2 implementation un- der the coupled per-query error budget. The dashed curve marks the same-precision qubit-Rz effective prefactor aLCU Rz (d, 3000, 10−6) from Eq. 121. Values of aLCU max above this reference prefactor indicate that the fully qudit implementa- tion can match or outperform the qubit baseline in total non- Clifford cost without requiring an embedded two-level qudit synthesis prefactor smaller than the corresponding qubit-Rz effective prefactor. Only prime local dimensions d are in- cluded in the fixed-encoding analysis.

FIG. 2. Non-Clifford resource comparison for the Regime 2 LCU/block-encoding implementation under the code-switching model, for εsim = 10−6 and t = 0.1. Panel (a) shows the ratio Rtot(d) = T qb tot(d)/T qd tot(d) as a function of the local dimension d. Panel (b) shows the corresponding total T-count estimates T qb tot(d) and T qd tot(d).

FIG. 3. Non-Clifford resource comparison for the Regime 2 LCU/block-encoding implementation under the code-switching model, for εsim = 10−6 and t = 3000. Panel (a) shows the ratio Rtot(d) = T qb tot(d)/T qd tot(d) as a function of the local dimension d. Panel (b) shows the corresponding total T-count estimates T qb tot(d) and T qd tot(d). The piecewise-constant structure and repeated crossover windows arise from the binary embedding.
